schools.summary: Heaven's Alleluia: 'and after these things I heard a great voice of much people in heaven, saying, Alleluia; Salvation, and glory, and honour, and power, unto the Lord our God' — in answer to the call to rejoice (18:20), a great multitude in heaven crying 'Alleluia' (Hallelujah, 'praise the LORD' — the first of four in this chapter, the only occurrences in the NT, the Hebrew shout of praise from the Psalms opening the heavenly celebration); they ascribing salvation, glory, honor, and power to God for his judgment on Babylon (the fourfold doxology magnifying God as the author of salvation and the sovereign whose judgments are glorious, the redeemed and heavenly host breaking into praise at God's righteous judgment) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

Victorinus and Bede: the great voice of the multitude in heaven crying Alleluia is the rejoicing of the church triumphant over the fall of the great city; heaven ascribes salvation, glory, honour, and power to God, magnifying his righteous judgment on Babylon — the heavenly celebration that the corrupt persecuting power is overthrown and God's people vindicated.

themes: a great voice of much people in heaven, saying, Alleluia
Salvation, and glory, and honour, and power, unto the Lord our God
heaven's Alleluia at Babylon's fall
heaven rejoicing at God's righteous judgment

schools.summary: The praise of God's judgment: 'for true and righteous are his judgments: for he hath judged the great whore, which did corrupt the earth with her fornication, and hath avenged the blood of his servants at her hand' — heaven praising God because his judgments are true and righteous (as 15:3; 16:7, faithful and just, heaven affirming the rightness of the judgment on Babylon); 'he hath judged the great whore, which did corrupt the earth with her fornication, and hath avenged the blood of his servants at her hand' the twofold ground (he judged her for corrupting the earth with her fornication and avenged the blood of his martyred servants, 18:24; 6:10, the two grounds — her corruption and her bloodguilt — justifying the praise, God having answered the martyrs' cry) — Barnes, Robertson, JFB.

themes: true and righteous are his judgments: for he hath judged the great whore, which did corrupt the earth with her fornication
hath avenged the blood of his servants at her hand
the praise of God's true judgment on the whore
God's justice vindicated and his martyred people avenged

schools.summary: The second Alleluia: 'and again they said, Alleluia. And her smoke rose up for ever and ever' — heaven uttering a second 'Alleluia,' and the smoke of Babylon's burning rising forever (the praise renewed, and the perpetual smoke marking the permanence of her judgment); the second 'Alleluia' intensifying the heavenly celebration (the praise of God for his judgment repeated and emphatic); 'her smoke rose up for ever and ever' the perpetual judgment (the smoke of her burning ascending forever, drawn from Isaiah 34:10, the perpetual desolation of Edom, the figure of the permanence and finality of her ruin, the rising smoke the eternal token of her judgment, occasioning unending praise) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: again they said, Alleluia. And her smoke rose up for ever and ever
the second Alleluia and the rising smoke of Babylon
the perpetual token of her final judgment
heaven's renewed and unending praise

schools.summary: The elders' worship: 'and the four and twenty elders and the four beasts fell down and worshipped God that sat on the throne, saying, Amen; Alleluia' — the twenty-four elders (the redeemed church) and the four living creatures falling down and worshiping God on the throne, sealing the heavenly praise with 'Amen; Alleluia' (the highest heavenly beings joining and confirming the celebration); 'fell down and worshipped God that sat on the throne' (the representatives of the redeemed and the heavenly host bowing in worship, the adoration of God for his judgment); 'Amen; Alleluia' the confirming praise (they confirm — 'Amen,' so be it, it is true — and praise — 'Alleluia,' the third in the chapter — God's judgment, the elders and creatures sealing the heavenly Alleluia with their assent and adoration) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the four and twenty elders and the four beasts fell down and worshipped God that sat on the throne, saying, Amen; Alleluia
the elders and living creatures worshipping with Amen and Alleluia
heaven sealing the celebration of God's judgment
the adoration of the redeemed and heavenly host

schools.summary: The call from the throne: 'and a voice came out of the throne, saying, Praise our God, all ye his servants, and ye that fear him, both small and great' — a voice from the throne (perhaps an angel or one of the living creatures) calling all God's servants to praise him (the summons to universal praise preparing for the great Alleluia of the marriage, vv.6-7); 'all ye his servants, and ye that fear him' the called worshipers (all who serve and fear God called to praise, the summons embracing the whole company of the faithful); 'both small and great' the universal scope (all the faithful, of every rank and degree, called to praise, none excluded, the great and the lowly alike joining in the praise of God, the comprehensive summons to worship) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: a voice came out of the throne, saying, Praise our God, all ye his servants, and ye that fear him, both small and great
the call from the throne to praise God
the universal summons to worship
all the faithful, small and great, called to praise

schools.summary: The great Alleluia: 'and I heard as it were the voice of a great multitude, and as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of mighty thunderings, saying, Alleluia: for the Lord God omnipotent reigneth' — a great multitude, like the roar of many waters and mighty thunder, crying 'Alleluia' (the fourth and climactic, the overwhelming, majestic praise of the whole heavenly host); the praise 'as the voice of many waters and mighty thunderings' (cf. 14:2) marking the immense volume and power of the heavenly chorus (the praise mighty as the sea and the storm); 'for the Lord God omnipotent reigneth' the ground (the climactic Alleluia grounded in God's reign, the Almighty having taken his power and reigning, 11:17, the establishment of God's unhindered, triumphant kingdom, after the fall of Babylon, the cause of the great praise) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: the voice of a great multitude... saying, Alleluia: for the Lord God omnipotent reigneth
the great Alleluia for God's reign
heaven's mighty celebration of God's triumphant reign
the praise mighty as many waters and thunder

schools.summary: The marriage of the Lamb: 'let us be glad and rejoice, and give honour to him: for the marriage of the Lamb is come, and his wife hath made herself ready' — the heavenly host rejoicing because the marriage of the Lamb has come (the long-awaited union of Christ, the Lamb, with his bride, the church, at hand, the figure of the consummated, glorious union of Christ and his people); 'the marriage of the Lamb' the consummation of the union between Christ and the church (the bride, 21:2, 9; cf. Ephesians 5:25-32; Matthew 22:2, the figure of the final, blessed union of the redeemed with their Lord, the cause of heaven's joy); 'his wife (bride) hath made herself ready' the prepared bride (the church having prepared herself for the marriage, her readiness — her purity and adornment, v.8 — the fruit of grace, the church made ready for her Lord) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the marriage of the Lamb is come, and his wife hath made herself ready
the marriage of the Lamb and the ready bride
the consummated, glorious union of Christ and his church
the cause of heaven's joy

schools.summary: The bride's fine linen: 'and to her was granted that she should be arrayed in fine linen, clean and white: for the fine linen is the righteousness of saints' — the bride granted (it is given to her, by grace) to be clothed in fine linen, clean and white (the pure white garment the bride's adornment, in pointed contrast to the harlot's purple and scarlet, 17:4; 18:16, the church's purity a gift of grace, 'granted'); 'clean and white (bright)' marking the purity, holiness, and righteousness of the bride (the spotless garment of the church made ready for her Lord); 'the fine linen is the righteousness (dikaiomata — righteous acts) of saints' the interpretation (the white linen explained as the righteousness of the saints, whether their righteous deeds — the fruit of grace, the visible holiness of the redeemed — or, more deeply, the righteousness of Christ imputed and worked in them, the bride's garment the righteousness in which the saints are arrayed, granted by grace) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: to her was granted that she should be arrayed in fine linen, clean and white
for the fine linen is the righteousness of saints
the bride's fine linen, the righteousness of saints
the church clothed by grace in the garment of righteousness

schools.summary: The marriage-supper beatitude: 'and he saith unto me, Write, Blessed are they which are called unto the marriage supper of the Lamb. And he saith unto me, These are the true sayings of God' — the angel bidding John write a beatitude: blessed are those invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb (the figure of the great feast of the consummated union, cf. Matthew 22:2-3; Luke 14:15-16, to be called — invited and admitted — to the Lamb's marriage feast being blessedness); 'the marriage supper of the Lamb' the joyful feast celebrating the union of Christ and the church (those called to it the redeemed who share the blessing of the consummation, the guests and the bride the saved); 'These are the true (genuine) sayings of God' the confirmation (the angel affirming that these words — the beatitude, and the visions — are the true, faithful words of God, the assurance that what is promised is sure, God's word true and to be fulfilled) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Blessed are they which are called unto the marriage supper of the Lamb
These are the true sayings of God
the beatitude on those called to the marriage supper
the redeemed admitted to the joyful feast of the Lamb

schools.summary: The refused worship and the spirit of prophecy: 'and I fell at his feet to worship him. And he said unto me, See thou do it not: I am thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren that have the testimony of Jesus: worship God: for the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy' — overwhelmed, John falling to worship the angel, but the angel forbidding it ('See thou do it not,' the angel refusing the worship that belongs to God alone, cf. 22:8-9); 'I am thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren that have the testimony of Jesus: worship God' (the angel a fellow servant of God with John and the brethren who hold the testimony of Jesus, directing the worship to God alone, angels not to be worshiped, a rebuke to angel-worship); 'the testimony of Jesus is the spirit (substance, animating principle) of prophecy' (the witness to Jesus the heart and soul of all prophecy, whether 'the testimony borne by Jesus' or 'the testimony concerning/about Jesus,' the meaning that the spirit and substance of prophecy is to testify of Christ, all true prophecy pointing to and bearing witness of Jesus) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: I fell at his feet to worship him. And he said unto me, See thou do it not... worship God
for the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy
John forbidden to worship the angel
the rebuke of angel-worship and the witness to Jesus the heart of prophecy

schools.summary: The rider Faithful and True: 'and I saw heaven opened, and behold a white horse; and he that sat upon him was called Faithful and True, and in righteousness he doth judge and make war' — heaven opening and revealing a rider on a white horse, called 'Faithful and True' (the rider Christ, the Word of God, KING OF KINGS, vv.13, 16, coming forth in triumph, the white horse the emblem of victory and conquest, 6:2, his title 'Faithful and True,' 3:14, marking his trustworthiness and truth); 'in righteousness he doth judge and make war' (unlike earthly conquerors, Christ judging and waging war in righteousness, all his acts of judgment and conquest just, he coming to judge and war against the wicked in perfect righteousness). Barnes and the futurist read the rider's going forth as Christ's coming to judge and conquer his enemies, many taking it of the second advent of Christ in glory to the final battle and judgment.

Gill reads the rider on the white horse as Christ coming forth in the latter part of the dispensation — through the progress of the gospel and the overthrow of antichrist, advancing to the last judgment; some read the conquest as the triumphant advance of the Word/gospel through the latter days rather than (or as well as) a single literal advent. The historicist frame takes the rider's war as the conquering course of Christ and his truth against the antichristian powers, distinct from the strictly futurist reading of a literal second coming to the final battle.

debates.question: What is the nature of the white-horse rider's coming forth to judge and make war?

sides.position: The literal second advent of Christ in glory to the final battle and judgment (futurist)
Christ's triumphant advance through the gospel and the overthrow of antichrist in the latter days, leading to the last judgment (historicist)

themes: I saw heaven opened, and behold a white horse; and he that sat upon him was called Faithful and True
in righteousness he doth judge and make war
the rider on the white horse, Faithful and True
the conquering Christ (the second advent, or the advance of his gospel)

schools.summary: The rider's majesty: 'his eyes were as a flame of fire, and on his head were many crowns; and he had a name written, that no man knew, but he himself' — the rider (Christ) having eyes like a flame of fire (1:14; 2:18, his penetrating, all-searching gaze, his searching omniscience), many crowns (diademata — diadems) on his head (marking his supreme, manifold sovereignty over all, many crowns for he is King of kings, v.16, the conqueror crowned with manifold sovereignty), and a secret name known only to himself; 'a name written, that no man knew, but he himself' (Christ having a name unknown to all but himself, the figure of his incomprehensible, transcendent nature, a depth of being and dignity in Christ beyond full human knowledge, the secret name marking his unsearchable majesty) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: his eyes were as a flame of fire, and on his head were many crowns
he had a name written, that no man knew, but he himself
the rider's flaming eyes, many crowns, and secret name
the all-searching, supremely sovereign, transcendent Lord

schools.summary: The vesture dipped in blood and the Word of God: 'and he was clothed with a vesture dipped in blood: and his name is called The Word of God' — the rider clothed in a robe dipped in blood, marking him as a victorious warrior; the dominant reading (Gill 'rather in the blood of his enemies, with which he appears stained,' Robertson noting Charles takes it as the blood of Christ's enemies, Wesley 'the blood of the enemies') taking the blood as that of his enemies, slain in judgment (Isaiah 63:1-3, the treading of the winepress, his garments stained red, the conqueror returning from the slaughter of his foes); 'his name is called The Word of God (the Logos)' the divine title (the rider the Word of God, John 1:1, 14; 1 John 1:1, the title affirming his deity and his role as the revealer and executor of God's will, the eternal Word now coming in judgment) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Ellicott.

debates.question: Whose blood is the rider's vesture dipped in?

sides.position: The blood of his enemies, slain in judgment (Isaiah 63:1-3, the treading of the winepress) — the dominant reading
His own blood — the atoning blood of the cross

themes: he was clothed with a vesture dipped in blood
his name is called The Word of God
the vesture dipped in blood and the name the Word of God
the conquering Word, his robe stained with the blood of his enemies (or his own atoning blood)

schools.summary: The armies of heaven: 'and the armies which were in heaven followed him upon white horses, clothed in fine linen, white and clean' — the heavenly armies following Christ, mounted on white horses and clothed in fine white linen (the host accompanying the conquering Christ, whether angels, or the redeemed saints, the bride's array, v.8, or both, following him to the battle); 'upon white horses' (sharing the white horse of the conqueror, v.11, the white marking victory and purity, the host triumphing with Christ); 'clothed in fine linen, white and clean' (the same fine linen as the bride, v.8, the righteousness of saints, the armies arrayed in purity and righteousness, whether the saints in their righteousness or the holy angels, following the Word of God in spotless white, not to fight — the victory Christ's alone, v.15 — but to share his triumph) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the armies which were in heaven followed him upon white horses, clothed in fine linen, white and clean
the armies of heaven following the rider
the saints and angels sharing the triumph of the conquering Word
arrayed in purity and righteousness

schools.summary: The sword and the winepress: 'and out of his mouth goeth a sharp sword, that with it he should smite the nations: and he shall rule them with a rod of iron: and he treadeth the winepress of the fierceness and wrath of Almighty God' — from Christ's mouth proceeding a sharp sword (the word of his judgment, 1:16; 2:12; Isaiah 11:4; 2 Thessalonians 2:8, with which he smites the nations, conquering not by carnal weapons but by the word of his power, his judgment going forth from his mouth); 'he shall rule (shepherd) them with a rod of iron' (Psalm 2:9; 12:5, Christ ruling the nations with firm, irresistible authority, the rod of iron marking his unbreakable sovereignty); 'he treadeth the winepress of the fierceness and wrath of Almighty God' (14:19-20; Isaiah 63:3, Christ treading the winepress of God's fierce wrath, executing the judgment of God on the wicked, treading them as grapes, the conquering Christ the executor of the Almighty's wrath) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Cyril: Christ treads the winepress of the fierceness and wrath of Almighty God — the conquering Lord executing the divine judgment on the wicked, smiting the nations with the sword of his word and ruling with the rod of iron; the Word of God coming forth as the appointed executor of God's wrath, treading down his enemies in the great judgment.

themes: out of his mouth goeth a sharp sword, that with it he should smite the nations
he treadeth the winepress of the fierceness and wrath of Almighty God
the sword of his mouth and the winepress of wrath
the Word executing the judgment of God on the wicked

schools.summary: The supreme title: 'and he hath on his vesture and on his thigh a name written, KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS' — the rider bearing, on his robe and on his thigh (where the sword hangs, or where the inscription would be visible and conspicuous, publicly displayed where all may see), the title 'KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS' (the open proclamation of his supreme sovereignty); 'KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS' the title of Christ's absolute supremacy (17:14; 1 Timothy 6:15; Deuteronomy 10:17, he sovereign over all kings and lords, the supreme Ruler of all, the conquering Christ openly proclaimed as the universal King and Lord) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

Cyril: the rider's name, KING OF KINGS AND LORD OF LORDS, openly proclaims Christ's absolute supremacy over all rulers and powers — the conquering Lord, sovereign over every king and lord, displayed in his universal kingship as he comes to judge and reign; the supreme title of the victorious Christ.

themes: he hath on his vesture and on his thigh a name written, KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS
the rider's name, KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS
the open proclamation of Christ's supreme sovereignty
the conquering Christ the universal King and Lord

schools.summary: The summons to the birds: 'and I saw an angel standing in the sun; and he cried with a loud voice, saying to all the fowls that fly in the midst of heaven, Come and gather yourselves together unto the supper of the great God' — an angel, standing in the sun (in a place of full visibility and glory), calling all the birds of prey to gather to 'the supper of the great God' (the grim, ironic feast — the birds summoned to feed on the slain enemies of Christ, vv.18, 21, drawn from Ezekiel 39:17-20, the summons of the birds to the sacrifice); 'an angel standing in the sun' the conspicuous herald (crying with a loud voice, far-heard, the herald of the coming slaughter); 'the supper of the great God' the grim feast (in dreadful contrast to the marriage supper of the Lamb, v.9, this the supper at which the birds feast on the corpses of the slain, the figure of the total destruction of Christ's enemies, the great God's supper the carnage of the wicked) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: an angel standing in the sun... saying to all the fowls... Come and gather yourselves together unto the supper of the great God
the angel calling the birds to the great supper of God
the grim feast on the slain enemies of Christ
the dreadful contrast to the marriage supper of the Lamb

schools.summary: The flesh of the slain: 'that ye may eat the flesh of kings, and the flesh of captains, and the flesh of mighty men, and the flesh of horses, and of them that sit on them, and the flesh of all men, both free and bond, both small and great' — the birds to eat the flesh of all the slain — kings, captains, mighty men, horses and riders, all men of every rank (the total slaughter of the enemies of Christ, embracing all classes); the slain including 'kings... captains... mighty men... horses... all men, both free and bond, both small and great' (the whole array of the antichristian host, high and low, destroyed, no rank escaping, the comprehensive ruin of Christ's enemies); 'both free and bond, both small and great' the universal slaughter (all classes without exception among the slain, the destruction total and embracing every degree, the carnage of the great supper sparing none of the enemies of Christ) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: that ye may eat the flesh of kings, and the flesh of captains, and the flesh of mighty men
the flesh of all men, both free and bond, both small and great
the birds feasting on the flesh of the slain
the total destruction of the antichristian host, sparing none

schools.summary: The final muster: 'and I saw the beast, and the kings of the earth, and their armies, gathered together to make war against him that sat on the horse, and against his army' — the beast (the persecuting power), the kings of the earth, and their armies gathered (as 16:14, 16, at Armageddon) to make war against Christ (the rider) and his heavenly army (the final muster of the antichristian powers for the last battle); 'the beast... the kings of the earth, and their armies' the assembled enemies (the whole antichristian confederacy, the beast and the ten kings, 17:12-14, assembling, the concentrated might of the enemies of Christ); 'gathered together to make war against him that sat on the horse, and against his army' (the antichristian powers presuming to war against Christ and his host, the futile, doomed assault on the King of kings, 17:14, the last battle which ends not in conflict but in the capture and destruction of the beast, v.20) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: I saw the beast, and the kings of the earth, and their armies, gathered together to make war against him that sat on the horse
the beast and kings gathered to war against Christ
the final muster of the antichristian powers
the doomed, futile assault on the King of kings

schools.summary: The beast and false prophet judged: 'and the beast was taken, and with him the false prophet that wrought miracles before him, with which he deceived them that had received the mark of the beast, and them that worshipped his image. These both were cast alive into a lake of fire burning with brimstone' — the beast and the false prophet (the second beast, 13:11-17, who deceived the world by his miracles) captured (the two great antichristian powers taken without a battle described, their overthrow decisive and swift); the false prophet 'deceived them that had received the mark of the beast, and... worshipped his image' (his miracles, 13:13-14, deceiving the world into beast-worship, his deception the ground of his judgment); 'these both were cast alive into a lake of fire burning with brimstone' (the beast and false prophet cast alive into the lake of fire, the first mention of the lake of fire, 20:10, 14-15, their final, decisive destruction, the two antichristian powers meeting their appointed end) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: the beast was taken, and with him the false prophet that wrought miracles before him
These both were cast alive into a lake of fire burning with brimstone
the beast and false prophet cast into the lake of fire
the decisive, final overthrow of the antichristian powers

schools.summary: The host slain by the sword of his mouth: 'and the remnant were slain with the sword of him that sat upon the horse, which sword proceeded out of his mouth: and all the fowls were filled with their flesh' — the rest (the armies of the beast and kings, v.19), after the beast and false prophet are taken, slain by the sword of Christ — the sword of his mouth (v.15; the word of his judgment, Christ destroying the antichristian host by the word of his power); 'which sword proceeded out of his mouth' (v.15; 1:16, Christ conquering and slaying not by carnal weapons but by the word of his judgment, his spoken word destroying his enemies, the victory wholly his); 'all the fowls were filled with their flesh' (the birds summoned to the great supper, vv.17-18, now filled with the flesh of the slain, the grim feast accomplished, the total destruction of Christ's enemies complete, the birds gorging on the carnage) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: the remnant were slain with the sword of him that sat upon the horse, which sword proceeded out of his mouth
all the fowls were filled with their flesh
the rest slain by the sword of Christ's mouth
Christ destroying his enemies by the word of his power
